pageTitle.value: Grégoire Courtine - The future of paralysis treatment - Rolex.org

pageCaption.value: Neuroscientist Grégoire Courtine is developing groundbreaking bioengineering technologies to treat spinal cord injury.

WaPageTitle.value: Grégoire Courtine - The future of paralysis treatment - Rolex.org

metadescription.value: Neurologist Grégoire Courtine is developing groundbreaking bioengineering technologies to restore mobility to paralysed people. More on rolex.org.

text.value: Neuroscientist Grégoire Courtine is developing groundbreaking bioengineering technologies to treat spinal cord injury. With the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, he hopes to restore mobility to paralysed people around the world.

As a physics and then neuroscience student, Grégoire Courtine dreamed of helping young people paralysed by spinal cord injury to recover. “Sport was very central in my life, movement was an essential feature to make me happy,” he says, so he found it unbearable to see others robbed of that capability.

The spinal cord carries signals – in the form of electrical impulses – from the brain to the rest of the body. When it is damaged or severed this can block the signals, causing paralysis below the injury. Around 20 million people worldwide have a spinal cord injury, which has a major impact on their quality of life.

Restoring movement after such injuries has been, as Courtine describes it, “one of the greatest quests in neurological medicine”, and, until recently, assumed by most to be something unattainable.

Previously, treatment of spinal cord injury focused on making patients comfortable and helping them adjust to living with paralysis. This was unacceptable to Courtine, a 2019 Laureate of the Rolex Awards for Enterprise who founded .NeuroRestore to develop ways to stimulate nerves in the spine artificially and help patients regain movement. With his research partner, neurosurgeon Jocelyne Bloch, he developed a technology called Epidural Electrical Stimulation – or EES – of the spinal cord.

Bloch envisions the spinal cord “like a highway, and after a spinal cord injury, this highway is interrupted”. The idea of EES was to circumvent this interruption. It delivers electrical signals to specific regions of the spinal cord below the injury, stimulating movement in limbs that were once paralysed.

With this technology, they succeeded in helping nine people with partial spinal cord lesions regain movement. Then in 2021, they implanted an EES device in Michel Roccati, a patient with a complete spinal cord lesion, for the first time. Having a complete spinal cord lesion means complete paralysis, but with the implant and some serious dedication and training, Roccati has been able to walk again. He describes the technology as “a message of hope”.

“That was a first victory,” says Bloch, but it was not the last. Over time, the .NeuroRestore team found that patients using EES were recovering increasingly more movement, indicating that their spinal neurons were regrowing. This was an astonishing discovery, as such recovery was once considered impossible.

There is no doubt that EES is life-changing, but Courtine wanted to go further. “It was very cumbersome to use in daily life,” he explains, because users had to press a button to trigger stimulation.

Courtine’s solution was to develop “a digital bridge that converts thoughts into action”, a revolutionary piece of implantable technology that reconnects the signals from the brain with the muscles of the lower body.

The bridge uses implants nested within the skull to measure brain signals, which are analysed by a computer and delivered to the EES device through electrodes implanted on the spinal cord. Combining these three pieces of cutting-edge technology, the digital bridge translates intentions from the brain into movement in the limbs, offering patients much more natural control.

After years of intensive development and testing, Courtine, Bloch and the .NeuroRestore team finally had a device ready to be implanted, but they needed a patient willing to be the first recipient of the new technology. Gert-Jan Oskam, who suffered a spinal cord injury in a cycling accident in 2011, volunteered.
If we don’t believe in the impossible, we can’t achieve what is possible.

“Doctors had told me, ‘It’s clear that you will never walk again’,” says Oskam, “I never accepted that answer.” His unwavering optimism and determination motivated him to get involved in this pioneering research.

Bloch completed the surgery successfully and then the team waited in suspense – would the device function? Would Oskam learn to use the device to control his movements with his thoughts once again?

Remarkably, within minutes of regaining consciousness, Oskam was already controlling an avatar on a computer through thought alone. Just days later, he could move his legs in the same way. “This was a true breakthrough,” says Courtine, and the results of the procedure have been published in the science journal Nature.

Courtine describes Oskam as “a true pioneer, courageous enough to be the first test pilot of a completely new medical technology”.
The team hopes one day soon the digital bridge will be available to anyone suffering from spinal cord injury.

Courtine received a Rolex Award to continue his work on the digital bridge. The Awards are a pillar of the Perpetual Planet Initiative, which supports many individuals and organizations in science-led efforts to improve life and preserve the natural world. The prize was a huge confidence boost for Courtine, but also an enormous responsibility, he says.

The next steps are to continue testing the technology and prepare it for commercialization. “It needs to be developed by an industry that brings it to the market, that distributes it around the world,” says Bloch. The team hopes one day soon the digital bridge will be available to anyone suffering from spinal cord injury.

The utility of the spinal stimulation technology does not stop there. Movement can be disrupted and limited by many different medical conditions, such as stroke or Parkinson’s disease. The .NeuroRestore team is already applying the technology to these new medical domains.

One patient with Parkinson’s has been treated with EES, with astounding results. Before the surgery, he fell several times a day, but when activated, the implant allowed him to walk comfortably, including in public settings, something previously not possible. Bloch sees treating Parkinson’s and other conditions with this technology as “the beginning of a new adventure”.

Despite all the achievements, .NeuroRestore is determined to develop the technology further. Courtine knows the digital bridge is remarkable, but says, “it’s not a cure for spinal cord injury.” His aim is that, with support from the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, the team can boost the regrowth of neurons they have seen in response to EES in the recipients so far. He says, “If we don’t believe in the impossible, we can't achieve what is possible.”

pageTitle.value: Mark Kendall - Medical alerts from a microwearable sticker - Rolex.org

pageCaption.value: From creating a pain-free vaccine patch, Australian scientist Mark Kendall is now developing microwearable devices that send early warnings when patients are experiencing events like heart attacks.

WaPageTitle.value: Mark Kendall - Medical alerts from a microwearable sticker - Rolex.org

metadescription.value: Scientist and 2012 Rolex Laureate Mark Kendall has produced microwearable sensors that provide critical medical information. Learn more on rolex.org.

pageCaption.value: By marrying textile science with robotics, Conor Walsh, an Irish biomedical engineer, and a team of experts at the Harvard Biodesign Lab are revolutionizing how patients worldwide recover from traumas such as stroke and learn to walk again.
